narrative:
Happy Mother’s Day! It’s a great day to get outside and enjoy with Mom if you are able. Sunshine prevails with a pleasantly warm afternoon. Monday will bring with it a few showers as temperatures cool down a bit. Another shower opportunity arrives for midweek. Both of these rainfall events will be quite light, but we will take what we can muster. Much of the week will feature below normal temperatures, before we warm-up noticeably heading into next weekend.

2027 Blue Ridge Photo Winners Announced

The votes are in, and the winning photos have been selected. The Grand Prize Winner is a fall photo by Jason Frye of Price Lake looking toward Grandfather Mountain. See this and all the other winners at our Photo Contest Page.

Get the free RWC Mobile App...: Apple and Android

Hi: 73
raw_text:
Show map Boone Right Now 64.9°F Today's forecast: Sunny followed by a few more afternoon clouds; Even warmer; WNW wind 5-10 mph. High 73° Station sponsor Forecast Current Graphics Archives Webcams Magnificent For Mom Last Updated Sun May 10 at 10:28AM by Scott Stephens Happy Mother’s Day! It’s a great day to get outside and enjoy with Mom if you are able. Sunshine prevails with a pleasantly warm afternoon. Monday will bring with it a few showers as temperatures cool down a bit. Another shower opportunity arrives for midweek. Both of these rainfall events will be quite light, but we will take what we can muster. Much of the week will feature below normal temperatures, before we warm-up noticeably heading into next weekend. 2027 Blue Ridge Photo Winners Announced The votes are in, and the winning photos have been selected. The Grand Prize Winner is a fall photo by Jason Frye of Price Lake looking toward Grandfather Mountain. See this and all the other winners at our Photo Contest Page. Get the free RWC Mobile App...: Apple and Android Hi: 73 Sunday Sunny followed by a few more afternoon clouds; Even warmer; WNW wind 5-10 mph Lo: 51 Sunday night Increasing cloudiness; Mild; Light NW wind Hi: 63 Monday A few showers into early afternoon; Cloudy, but decreasing late-day; Cooler; Light NW wind Lo: 39 Monday night Becoming clear; Chilly; A few valley frost patches by daybreak; Light NW wind becoming calm Hi: 65 Tuesday Abundant sunshine, but on the cool side; Calm wind becoming light south Lo: 45 Tuesday night Becoming partly cloudy; Seasonable; Light SW wind Hi: 67 Wednesday Mostly cloudy; Scattered showers Lo: 44 Wednesday night Mostly cloudy; A leftover evening shower; Cool Hi: 62 Thursday Clouds early; Becoming mostly sunny; Unseasonably cool Lo: 39 Thursday night Generally clear; Chilly; Few valley frost patches by sunup Friday Extended forecast is only available for RaysWeather.com supporters. Subscribe Saturday Extended forecast is only available for RaysWeather.com supporters. Subscribe Forecast Discussion We are situated in a pre-frontal environment for Mother’s Day, and that will place western North Carolina and Southwest Virginia in a pleasantly warm and quiet air mass today. As the front slips through early Monday, showers will be around into early Monday afternoon, and temperatures will be cooler. Under clear skies Tuesday morning, it will be chilly, although frost potential looks limited to deeper, sheltered valleys. Tuesday will be bright, sunny but seasonably cool. Another cold front moves through on Wednesday with a couple of showers. Again, the operative word on rainfall amounts is “light”. Another noticeable cooldown follows for Thursday. Once we get to Friday and beyond, high pressure ridging at the surface and aloft will regain command of our weather. That means warming temperatures and a continuation of dry/drought conditions heading into the following weekend. Detailed forecast discussion and analysis is only available to RaysWeather supporters. Subscribe Log in Scott Stephens Chief Meteorologist PHOTO OF THE DAY CLICK TO ENLARGE Enjoying the overcast view from the Jumpin Off overlook. Submitted by Allison Powers. All Photos Submit Photo Latest Insights On This Date: The May 1992 Snowstorm It is a story that long-time WNC residents have heard more than a few times in their lives. The legendary spring blizzard of May 6-8, 1992 that supposedly dropped nearly 60 inches of snow on Mount Pisgah, stranding 130+ guests and staff at the Pisgah Inn without electricity or hot water. The 60-inch total is almost certainly the measurement of a snow drift (winds gusted above 50 mph, as recounted in the image below), but totals in the 30 to 40 inch range are feasible, as Mount Mitchell's 30-inch report is the highest verifiable one from this event. Not to mention, our own Scott Stephens ventured up top on the 7th, and recalls 30 to 40 inches on the ground that day, as well as plenty of thunder and lightning on the night of the 6th. These numbers are ludicrous for May! Sure, Mount Mitchell has recorded over 2 feet of snow more than a few times over the years, but for a total this high to occur closer to Memorial Day than March 1st is truly remarkable. To the west, Mount LeConte recorded 17 inches of snow, for its largest May snowfall on record. As the clipping above states, Asheville's 0.2 inches of snow was the first ever instance of May snowfall. Beech Mountain and Grandfather Mountain recieved more than the "five to six inches" noted in the above report, with official records closer to 8 to 12 inches. In Boone, just enough snow to make the roads slick for a while fell, but not enough to shut down the town. Even the South Carolina mountains got in on the action, with Caesar's Head recording 1.5 inches of snow! The abnormal system was the result of an unusually strong and cold upper-level low pressure system, the same sort of system that dropped 9+ inches on high elevations back around Veteran's Day last November. The difference with the May 1992 system is that the timing is even further outside of the usual timing for snow in WNC, meaning the system must be more anomalous than our November snowmaker, and must move slowly to produce such high precipitation amounts. 30+ inch totals at Mitchell and Pisgah were recorded despite reports that precipitation flipped back and forth from rain to snow occasionally, showing the fickle nature of the springtime snowfall. This event's late timing brought many downed trees and power lines to elevations that recieved notable snowfall, some due to wind, but some due to the weight of snow on leafed-out trees! Up at the Pisgah Inn above 5,000 feet, most trees would still be bare or just beginning to leaf out in early May, but plenty of leaves exist below 4000 feet or so, allowing for the unusual situation where snow has more surface area to collect on trees and weigh them down. This can be seen in the second photograph below, as branches sag. The Los Angeles Times has an archived article regarding the legendary snowfall atop Mt. Pisgah, which you can read for yourself here. The story reports trapped school buses from trees and lines on the roads, as well as Floridian tourists escorted down the mountain by Haywood County police! How Unusual Is Spring Snow? The degree of snowfall observed at high elevations in May 1992 is about as unusual as it gets for spring snow. Heavy April snows are not unheard of, although they are not common. Heavy snows beyond mid-March are much less surprising. Let's use Mount Mitchell as our guinea pig. Put this way: the average final date of snowfall on Mt. Mitchell since 1980 is April 10th. The earliest final snowfall is February 13th, and the latest is May 14th. Snowfall of greater than a foot has been observed 6 times in April and May, with 1992 the only instance of such a total later than April 12th. More simply put: it isn't especially unusual for early May to send a few flakes the way of the 6000 foot peaks, but it is a once-in-a-lifetime, unicorn event for the season's heaviest snowfall to occur in early May. 27.0" is tied for the 24th-heaviest 3-day snowfall on Mitchell... but only one event that occurred after the spring solstice exceeded that, with 35.0" between April 3rd and 5th, 1987. This event was so out of the ordinary for the time of year, that the 3-day total more than doubles the total from the next-highest snowfall after Tax Day on Mt. Mitchell (11.0", 4/24-26/2005). Those are the only two events to reach double digits after April 15th. Further to the west, Mount LeConte holds the title for the latest snowfall of any sort recorded in the Southern Appalachians. If you have ever made the trek to the summit, you may have noticed the decades of weather records available on the outside of the LeConte Lodge. Those records note that the latest snow on record occurred June 1st, 1983, in the form of flurries. Blogger Tom Layton dug through old newspaper clippings and discovered this piece of the Knoxville News Sentinel reporting snow on LeConte on June 2, 1956. Whether that is true or not is likely impossible to verify for certain, but either way it shows that June snow is doable at the highest of heights in North Carolina and Tennessee. Enjoy this more recent image of May snowfall, atop one of the supply-carrying llamas on LeConte in 2014. The rarity of the May 1992 snowstorm cannot be overstated, and who knows when we may see an event anywhere near that level of absurdity. Forget most impressive snowstorms, this is one of the wildest weather events of any kind in the history of the state of North Carolina. May 07, 2026 WWR? The Parkway Foundation There’s nothing quite like the Blue Ridge Parkway, and the Parkway Foundation plays a huge role in keeping it beautiful for everyone to enjoy. Join us as we sit down with the Foundation’s CEO, Carolyn, to talk about the programs and projects making a difference along the Parkway — and how you can help preserve the place we all love. #ExploreWNC May 08, 2026 April 2026 Recap The Main Story They say that "April showers bring May flowers." If that is the case, what does April drought bring? Well, we may (pun intended) be about to find out, because April was about as dry as can be for many folks around here. We'll get further into those details later. By and large, it was a quiet month, characterized by long stretches of dry, warm weather with a handful of low temperatures exceeding the average high temperature for the date. Boone notched 16 highs of 70 degrees or more, in a month where the average high does not exceed 70 degrees at all (peaking with an average of 66F on the 30th). While most stayed dry or saw a brief shower on April 1st, the most notable moment of the month for Deep Gap unfolded as a prolific hail storm rumbled through the area. Locals reported noticeable hail accumulation, with largely pea to marble sized hail coating the ground in a few select spots. A "cooler" period marked the first third of the month, although it still featured multiple days at or above average. From the 10th on to the 18th... warm and dry. Downtown Asheville recorded its warmest temperature of 2026 to date on the 15th at 89F. There was no rain throughout this stretch of the month. The graph below tells the story, with the big swings of February and March a thing of the past: the coldest and warmest highs were 27 degrees apart, whereas in March they were 50 degrees apart (87F and 37F). Perhaps the most impressive thing about this chart is not the string of 80s mid-month, but the fact that there was not a single freeze. This marks the second consecutive April that the Asheville airport did not record a freeze, which broke a streak of 12 consecutive April "last freezes." In fact, dating back to 1873, only 15 last freezes have occurred in March. Barring a May freeze (which is not unheard of, certainly), we will move that number to 16. The only major cooldown of the month occurred around April 20th, when Beech Mountain residents reported brief flurries mixed in with a night of freezing fog and rime ice that briefly restored the winter feel. Mount Mitchell dropped to 24 degrees at noontime on the 19th, the second coldest daily low of the month. April went out with two beneficial rains, moving eastward across the area the mornings of the 28th and the 30th. Many locations totaled 0.5 to 1.5 inches of rain from those two events... the majority of April rains. Local Details

Beech Mtn 61.1 53.3 7.8 42.5 36.4 6.1 51.8 44.9 7.0
Boone 69.0 61.7 7.3 46.9 36.9 10.0 58.0 49.3 8.7
Burnsville 72.7 66.0 6.7 48.0 41.9 6.1 60.4 54.0 6.4
Galax 71.3 64.7 6.6 45.7 39.4 6.3 58.5 52.1 6.5
Hickory 76.9 70.0 6.9 52.4 47.7 4.7 64.7 58.9 5.8
Jefferson 69.0 63.1 5.9 47.2 36.3 10.9 58.1 49.7 8.4
Lenoir 78.9 70.1 8.8 47.3 45.0 2.3 63.1 57.6 5.6
Marshall 73.0 68.8 4.2 46.8 38.9 8.5 59.9 53.6 6.4
Morganton 80.0 70.6 9.4 48.2 44.9 3.3 64.1 57.8 6.3
Mt. Mitchell 57.5 50.2 7.3 39.2 32.0 7.2 48.4 41.1 7.3
North Wilkesboro 77.1 69.9 7.2 49.5 43.6 5.9 63.3 56.8 6.6
Sparta 69.3 63.4 5.9 43.5 39.8 3.7 56.4 51.6 4.8
Waynesville 73.5 65.6 7.9 46.5 39.6 6.9 60.0 52.6 7.4
Wytheville 72.9 64.5 8.4 46.0 39.2 6.8 59.5 51.9 7.6
Warm, warm, warm. No other way to slice it, April was well above normal. In fact, it was the warmest April on record at six local climate sites: Boone, Beech Mountain, Hot Springs, Old Fort, Galax, and Hickory. Many other locations achieved top 5 or top 10 warmest Aprils. The region ran 4 to 8 degrees above normal. Highs of 91.4 on the 15th and 18th at the Morganton-Lenoir Airport were the warmest of any station in the Ray's Weather coverage area all month. Below is a map of temperature anomalies for the Contentintal U.S. for April.

Boone 1.78 5.52 32.2 8 0.0

Galax 1.60 4.07 39.3 3 0.0

Sparta 2.18 5.36 40.7 8 0.0
Waynesville 1.46 4.54 32.2 10 0.0
Wytheville 1.95 3.75 52.0 7 0.0
Of course, as everyone is well aware: it is dry. April did not help this, with the majority of stations recieveing less than half of their usual April rainfall. North Wilkesboro wins the badge of honor (or shame) for least rain in the month of April in Western N.C. and Virginia. Just 0.66" for an entire month! Most of that came from the aforementioned morning rains during the final week of the month. Streams and rivers are running slow, and lakes and reservoirs are showing plenty of lakebed now. We've flipped into a more active pattern to start May, but we will need more help to dig out of the deficit, rain-wise. May 05, 2026 See all Golf-O-Meter 0 Golfballs Only ducks or polar bears. Good day for knitting. Maybe, if you are brave. Worth taking a chance. A good day to be outside. Bosses, you'd better stand at the door. (Or will bosses be first out the door?) CONTACT US Privacy Policy Terms and Conditions Contact Us
